title: Employment discrimination based on sexual orientation or gender identity
subtitle: Includes discrimination in hiring, promotion, termination and harassment at work. Only legal status considered, not enforcement.
caption:
Employment discrimination based on sexual orientation or gender identity, 2025

Includes discrimination in hiring, promotion, termination and harassment at work. Only legal status considered, not enforcement.

This world map displays the legal status of employment discrimination protections based on sexual orientation or gender identity across countries in 2025. The visualization uses a categorical color scale to distinguish between nations offering comprehensive protections, partial protections, or no protections at all. The legend defines seven distinct categories: blue for countries protecting both sexual orientation and gender identity, green for gender identity only, light green for sexual orientation only, yellow for protections that vary by region, orange for ambiguous status, red for no protections, and grey for no data. The most extensive legal coverage, protecting both sexual orientation and gender identity, is visible across North America, including the United States and Canada, as well as throughout South America, with the exception of Suriname and Guyana. This blue category also encompasses Australia, New Zealand, and a significant portion of Western and Northern Europe, including the United Kingdom, France, Germany, and the Nordic countries. In contrast, the red category indicating no protections dominates the map geographically, covering the entirety of Russia, China, and the Middle East, along with most of Africa and parts of Southeast Asia. Countries with partial protections appear in specific clusters; India and several nations in Southern Africa fall into the green band protecting gender identity only, while Mexico and parts of Central America appear in the yellow band where protections vary by region. The grey areas denoting no data are concentrated in Central Africa and parts of the Pacific. The distribution shows a clear divide where comprehensive legal frameworks are concentrated in the Americas and Europe, while the absence of explicit employment protections is the prevailing legal status across Asia and Africa.
